runs.note: Runtime selected scalar path. Clang auto-vectorized scalar loops to NEON.

filter_bench.note: AVX2 path not available on ARM; the avx2 row reflects the scalar fallback the runtime selected.

aggregate_bench.note: Scalar loop auto-vectorized to NEON; both rows are effectively the same code path on ARM.

runs.note: Smoke bench from CI. 1M rows fits in L2 on Intel Xeon Platinum 8272CL (the typical GH-hosted runner CPU), so this is the cache-hot best case. The AVX2 path is the hand-written intrinsic kernel.

scan_bench.note: Full pipeline scan_bench includes pipeline plumbing and bitmap allocation per batch; the raw filter and aggregate kernels are faster (see below).

aggregate_bench.note: gcc -O3 auto-vectorizes the scalar sum loop into AVX2; that's why the gap is much smaller than for the filter kernel, which has a bitmap-pack step the auto-vectorizer can't synthesize.

honest_4_1b_per_sec_claim.achieved_for_full_pipeline_scan_at_1M_ro: no (1.312 B v/s); per-batch bitmap allocation and pipeline overhead bound the full pipeline. The raw filter kernel hits 7.8 B v/s in isolation.
honest_4_1b_per_sec_claim.achieved_at_100M_rows_on_apple_silicon_a: no (1.34 B v/s); 400 MB working set is DRAM-bound, not cache-resident
honest_4_1b_per_sec_claim.reasoning: The 4.1B v/s target is a kernel-level number on cache-resident input. The CI numbers above confirm that target on a single GitHub-hosted Skylake-class core for both the filter and the aggregate kernels at 1M rows (4 MB int32, comfortably in L2 cache). On 100M-row inputs (400 MB, well outside any cache) DRAM bandwidth becomes the bottleneck and throughput drops to ~1.3 B v/s on both architectures. CI bench-smoke asserts a 100M v/s floor; current numbers exceed it by 13x.
